dataset_name: Omnus Labs Feasibility Gold Set Cleaned

vignettes.text:
A 73-year-old man with low-count monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis diagnosed three years ago returns for routine surveillance. Serial complete blood counts and flow cytometry every six months have remained stable. Current absolute lymphocyte count is 3.1 × 10^9/L with a small CD5-positive, CD23-positive, CD20-dim monoclonal B-cell population measuring 0.38 × 10^9/L by flow cytometry. Hemoglobin is 13.8 g/dL and platelet count is 195 × 10^9/L. He has no lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, or clinical features of chronic lymphocytic leukemia.
He denies fatigue, night sweats, or infections. Physical examination is unremarkable. Current medications include tamsulosin and atorvastatin.
This represents stable low-count monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis with no evidence of progression to chronic lymphocytic leukemia or other lymphoproliferative disorder. Continued surveillance with complete blood count and flow cytometry in six to twelve months is appropriate.

A 68-year-old man with monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis returns for surveillance. He was diagnosed four years ago with a small CD5-positive, CD23-positive monoclonal B-cell population. His absolute lymphocyte count has slowly risen from 3.8 × 10^9/L to 5.9 × 10^9/L over the past 18 months. Flow cytometry continues to show a CLL-like phenotype comprising approximately 35% of lymphocytes. Hemoglobin and platelet count remain normal. He has no palpable lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly and reports no fatigue, night sweats, or infections.
Current medications include atorvastatin and losartan. This represents slowly progressive monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis approaching the threshold for chronic lymphocytic leukemia by absolute lymphocyte count criteria, though he remains asymptomatic without clinical features of CLL. Continued surveillance with complete blood count every six months is appropriate, with hematology referral for formal CLL staging if the lymphocyte count continues to rise or symptoms develop.

A 64-year-old man with monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis diagnosed three years ago returns for surveillance. His absolute lymphocyte count has remained stable between 3.5 and 4.1 × 10^9/L. Recent CT imaging performed for unrelated reasons identified small mediastinal and axillary lymph nodes measuring up to 1.2 cm. He denies B symptoms, fatigue, or infections.
Physical examination shows no palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. Flow cytometry continues to demonstrate a small CD5-positive, CD23-positive monoclonal B-cell population. Hemoglobin and platelet count are normal. Current medications include tamsulosin and metformin.
This represents low-count monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis with incidental small lymphadenopathy on imaging. While still consistent with MBL, the new radiographic findings warrant closer clinical and radiographic surveillance. Repeat CT imaging in six to twelve months and hematology evaluation if nodes enlarge or symptoms develop is recommended.

entities.verbatim: small mediastinal and axillary lymph nodes

vignettes.text:
A 70-year-old man with monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis diagnosed five years ago returns for surveillance. His absolute lymphocyte count has gradually increased from 3.2 × 10^9/L to 6.8 × 10^9/L over the past two years. Flow cytometry continues to show a CD5-positive, CD23-positive CLL-like phenotype. He remains asymptomatic with no lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, or cytopenias. Hemoglobin is 13.5 g/dL and platelet count is 175 × 10^9/L.
He denies fatigue, night sweats, or infections. Physical examination is normal. Current medications include atorvastatin and tamsulosin.
This case now meets absolute lymphocyte count criteria for chronic lymphocytic leukemia, though he remains asymptomatic without clinical stigmata of CLL. Transition from MBL surveillance to formal CLL staging and risk stratification with fluorescence in situ hybridization and IGHV mutation status is recommended.

A 76-year-old man with a five-year history of IgG kappa monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance returns for routine surveillance. Serial serum protein electrophoresis and immunofixation over the past three years have shown a stable M-spike ranging from 1.08 to 1.25 g/dL. The most recent value is 1.15 g/dL. Serum free light chain ratio remains normal at 0.92. Hemoglobin is stable at 13.4 g/dL, calcium is 9.3 mg/dL, and creatinine is 1.1 mg/dL with no evidence of hypercalcemia, renal insufficiency, anemia, or bone lesions.
He denies bone pain, fatigue, or constitutional symptoms. Physical examination is unremarkable with no lymphadenopathy or organomegaly. Current medications include metoprolol, atorvastatin, and low-dose aspirin for coronary artery disease.
This represents stable IgG kappa MGUS with no features concerning for progression to multiple myeloma or related plasma cell disorder. Continued surveillance with serum protein electrophoresis and free light chains in 12 months is recommended unless new symptoms develop.

A 61-year-old woman with IgA lambda monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance diagnosed 20 months ago returns for surveillance. Her M-spike has risen from 0.75 g/dL at diagnosis to 1.05 g/dL six months ago and is now 1.6 g/dL on the most recent serum protein electrophoresis. Serum free light chain ratio is mildly abnormal at 2.8. Hemoglobin remains stable at 12.1 g/dL, calcium is 9.4 mg/dL, and creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL. She reports no new bone pain, fatigue, or weight loss.
Physical examination is normal without lymphadenopathy or organomegaly. A recent low-dose whole-body CT showed no lytic lesions. Current medications include metformin for type 2 diabetes and losartan for hypertension.
The rising monoclonal protein raises concern for possible progression toward smoldering myeloma. Bone marrow biopsy with cytogenetics and FISH is being discussed to better characterize the plasma cell burden and risk stratification.

entities.verbatim: IgA lambda monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance

vignettes.text:
A 69-year-old woman with a three-year history of light-chain monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance returns for surveillance. At diagnosis, her involved free light chain (lambda) was 42 mg/L with a ratio of 3.8. On the most recent testing, the involved free light chain has risen to 78 mg/L with a ratio of 5.2. No intact monoclonal protein is detectable by serum protein electrophoresis or immunofixation. Hemoglobin is stable at 12.6 g/dL, calcium is 9.2 mg/dL, and creatinine is 0.95 mg/dL.
She denies bone pain, fatigue, or weight loss. Physical examination is normal. A 24-hour urine protein electrophoresis shows no significant light chain proteinuria. Current medications include amlodipine and atorvastatin.
This represents light-chain MGUS with a slowly rising involved free light chain level. Although still within MGUS parameters, closer surveillance with repeat free light chains in six months and consideration of bone marrow biopsy if further progression occurs is recommended.

entities.verbatim: light-chain monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance

vignettes.text:
A 74-year-old man with IgG kappa MGUS diagnosed seven years ago returns for annual surveillance. His M-spike has been stable between 0.9 and 1.1 g/dL for the past four years but has now increased to 1.45 g/dL. Serum free light chain ratio remains normal. Hemoglobin is 13.1 g/dL, calcium is 9.4 mg/dL, and creatinine is stable at 1.2 mg/dL. He has no new bone pain or constitutional symptoms.
Physical examination shows no lymphadenopathy or organomegaly. A recent skeletal survey showed no lytic lesions. Current medications include metoprolol, aspirin, and tamsulosin.
Although the M-spike remains below the 3 g/dL threshold and there are no CRAB features, the recent rise warrants closer monitoring. Repeat serum protein electrophoresis and free light chains in six months, with bone marrow biopsy to be considered if further progression occurs, is planned.

A 67-year-old man with IgG kappa MGUS diagnosed five years ago returns for surveillance. His M-spike has slowly risen from 0.8 g/dL at diagnosis to 1.35 g/dL on the most recent serum protein electrophoresis. Hemoglobin has declined slightly from 13.8 g/dL to 12.4 g/dL over the past year. Calcium and creatinine remain normal, and he denies bone pain or weight loss.
Physical examination is unremarkable. A recent skeletal survey showed no lytic lesions. Serum free light chain ratio is normal. Current medications include amlodipine, atorvastatin, and aspirin.
Although still meeting criteria for MGUS, the combination of rising M-spike and new mild anemia raises concern for possible progression toward smoldering myeloma. Bone marrow biopsy with cytogenetics and FISH is being recommended for better risk stratification and to guide surveillance intensity.

A 71-year-old woman with long-standing IgG lambda MGUS returns after developing new mid-back pain. Her M-spike has been stable at approximately 1.0 g/dL for the past six years. Recent serum protein electrophoresis shows no significant change. However, given the new back pain, imaging and laboratory evaluation were performed. MRI of the spine shows degenerative changes without focal lesions. Hemoglobin is stable at 12.1 g/dL, calcium is normal, and creatinine is stable.
She denies night sweats or weight loss. Physical examination shows no focal tenderness or neurologic deficits. Current medications include ibuprofen as needed for back pain and a statin.
This represents stable MGUS with new back pain most likely musculoskeletal in origin. However, given the history of MGUS, repeat imaging and laboratory studies in three to six months, or sooner if symptoms worsen, are planned to ensure no occult progression.

A 66-year-old man with IgG kappa MGUS diagnosed four years ago returns after new laboratory abnormalities were noted by his primary care physician. His M-spike has been stable at 1.1–1.2 g/dL. However, recent labs show calcium of 10.8 mg/dL (previously normal) and creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.0 mg/dL). He denies bone pain, polyuria, or constipation.
Physical examination is unremarkable. Serum protein electrophoresis shows the M-spike unchanged at 1.15 g/dL. Serum free light chain ratio is normal. A skeletal survey shows no lytic lesions. Current medications include lisinopril and metformin.
Although the M-spike is stable, the new hypercalcemia and rising creatinine raise concern for possible progression of the plasma cell disorder. Urgent evaluation with repeat calcium, hydration, and consideration of bone marrow biopsy is planned.

A 73-year-old man with known IgG kappa MGUS for six years returns with new anemia. His M-spike has been stable at 0.9–1.1 g/dL. However, hemoglobin has declined from 13.2 g/dL to 10.1 g/dL over the past eight months. He denies bleeding, weight loss, or bone pain. Iron studies show ferritin 165 ng/mL and normal transferrin saturation. Vitamin B12 and folate are normal.
Physical examination shows no splenomegaly. Serum protein electrophoresis confirms the M-spike unchanged at 1.05 g/dL. Peripheral smear shows no rouleaux or abnormal plasma cells. Current medications include metoprolol and atorvastatin.
This represents known stable MGUS with new unexplained anemia. While the M-spike has not progressed, the anemia raises concern for possible evolution to smoldering or active myeloma versus a concurrent process such as myelodysplastic syndrome. Bone marrow biopsy is recommended.

A 64-year-old woman with chronic lymphocytic leukemia on acalabrutinib for two years presents with new fatigue and jaundice. Her CLL has been well controlled with absolute lymphocyte count stable at 8–12 × 10^9/L. She now has hemoglobin 7.8 g/dL, reticulocyte count 11%, LDH 420 U/L, haptoglobin <10 mg/dL, and a positive direct antiglobulin test (IgG). She denies fever or bleeding.
Physical examination shows scleral icterus and mild splenomegaly. She has no palpable lymphadenopathy. Current medications include acalabrutinib 100 mg twice daily and low-dose aspirin.
This represents chronic lymphocytic leukemia on BTK inhibitor therapy complicated by new warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia. This is a known complication of CLL and BTK inhibitor therapy. Corticosteroid therapy and consideration of BTK inhibitor dose adjustment or switch are being discussed.

A 52-year-old man with chronic phase CML on dasatinib returns after missing several doses due to gastrointestinal side effects and cost concerns. His most recent BCR-ABL1 IS level has risen from 0.05% six months ago to 1.8% currently. He reports good energy but admits to taking dasatinib only five to six days per week for the past four months. He denies bleeding or pleural effusion symptoms.
Physical examination is normal. Complete blood count shows white blood cell count 12.4 × 10^9/L with left shift but no blasts. Current medications include dasatinib 100 mg daily and omeprazole.
This represents chronic myeloid leukemia with rising BCR-ABL1 level likely due to suboptimal adherence to dasatinib. Restarting daily dosing, addressing side effects and cost barriers, and close molecular monitoring are planned. Mutation analysis will be sent if response does not improve.

A 67-year-old man with a five-year history of JAK2-positive polycythemia vera on hydroxyurea returns with new mild anemia and rising M-spike. His hematocrit has been well controlled on hydroxyurea. Recent serum protein electrophoresis performed for fatigue workup incidentally revealed a new IgA lambda M-spike of 0.6 g/dL. Hemoglobin is now 11.2 g/dL (previously 14.5 g/dL). He denies bone pain or weight loss.
Physical examination shows no splenomegaly. Calcium and creatinine are normal. Current medications include hydroxyurea 500 mg daily and low-dose aspirin.
This represents polycythemia vera with a new concurrent IgA MGUS. The mild anemia may be related to hydroxyurea or early progression of the plasma cell disorder. Close monitoring of both the MPN and the new monoclonal protein with repeat serum protein electrophoresis in three to six months is planned.

A 59-year-old man with chronic phase chronic myeloid leukemia diagnosed in 2022 returns for routine follow-up on nilotinib. He was switched from imatinib to nilotinib 300 mg twice daily approximately 2.5 years ago due to suboptimal response. His most recent BCR-ABL1 p210 by international scale PCR is 0.07%, consistent with stable major molecular response for over 12 months. He reports good energy and adherence to therapy.
He denies rash, muscle cramps, pleural effusion symptoms, or significant bleeding. Complete blood count shows white blood cell count 4.8 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 13.9 g/dL, and platelet count 108 × 10^9/L, all stable compared with prior values. Physical examination is normal.
Current medications include nilotinib 300 mg twice daily, low-dose aspirin, and metoprolol. This represents an excellent and stable response to nilotinib with good tolerability. Continued therapy at the current dose with BCR-ABL1 monitoring every three months is recommended.

entities.verbatim: BCR-ABL1 p210 by international scale PCR

vignettes.text:
A 66-year-old woman with chronic lymphocytic leukemia diagnosed in 2020 returns for follow-up on acalabrutinib. She was started on acalabrutinib 100 mg twice daily 22 months ago after progression on observation. Her absolute lymphocyte count has decreased from a peak of 68 × 10^9/L to 14 × 10^9/L and has been stable for the past six months. She reports good energy and adherence.
She denies bleeding, significant bruising, arthralgias, or palpitations. Physical examination shows no palpable lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. Complete blood count demonstrates white blood cell count 18.2 × 10^9/L with absolute lymphocyte count 13.8 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, and platelet count 142 × 10^9/L. Current medications include acalabrutinib 100 mg twice daily and low-dose aspirin.
This represents an excellent partial response to acalabrutinib with good tolerability and no significant adverse effects to date. Continued therapy with routine laboratory monitoring every three months is recommended.

A 61-year-old man with polycythemia vera diagnosed in 2019 returns for routine follow-up on hydroxyurea. He was started on hydroxyurea 500 mg daily three years ago after recurrent need for phlebotomy. His hematocrit has been well controlled between 42% and 45% for the past 18 months, and he has required only one phlebotomy in the past year. He reports good tolerance with no mouth ulcers, gastrointestinal upset, or cytopenias.
Physical examination shows no splenomegaly. Laboratory studies demonstrate hematocrit 43.8%, white blood cell count 6.8 × 10^9/L, and platelet count 312 × 10^9/L. Current medications include hydroxyurea 500 mg daily and low-dose aspirin.
This represents stable, well-controlled polycythemia vera on low-dose hydroxyurea with reduced phlebotomy requirement and good tolerability. Continued current therapy with hematocrit monitoring every three months and JAK2 allele burden assessment as clinically indicated is planned.

A 68-year-old man with chronic lymphocytic leukemia returns for follow-up on zanubrutinib. He was started on zanubrutinib 160 mg twice daily 18 months ago after developing atrial fibrillation on ibrutinib. His absolute lymphocyte count has decreased from 45 × 10^9/L to 9 × 10^9/L and has been stable for the past nine months. He reports good energy and no bleeding or significant bruising.
He has had no recurrence of atrial fibrillation since switching agents and denies arthralgias or rash. Physical examination shows no lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. Complete blood count demonstrates white blood cell count 12.4 × 10^9/L with absolute lymphocyte count 8.9 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, and platelet count 138 × 10^9/L.
Current medications include zanubrutinib 160 mg twice daily, apixaban, and metoprolol. This represents an excellent response to zanubrutinib with good tolerability and resolution of prior ibrutinib-related atrial fibrillation. Continued therapy with routine monitoring is recommended.

A 59-year-old man with primary myelofibrosis returns for follow-up on ruxolitinib. He was started on ruxolitinib 15 mg twice daily two years ago for symptomatic splenomegaly and constitutional symptoms. His spleen size has decreased from 8 cm to 3 cm below the costal margin, and he reports resolution of night sweats and improved energy. He has had no significant cytopenias requiring dose adjustment.
Physical examination confirms a spleen palpable 3 cm below the left costal margin. Laboratory studies show white blood cell count 8.5 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, and platelet count 165 × 10^9/L, all stable. Current medications include ruxolitinib 15 mg twice daily and low-dose aspirin.
This represents a good symptomatic and splenic response to ruxolitinib with stable blood counts and good tolerability. Continued therapy at the current dose with routine laboratory and clinical monitoring every three months is recommended.

A 64-year-old woman with multiple myeloma status post autologous stem cell transplant three years ago returns for routine follow-up on lenalidomide maintenance. She has been on lenalidomide 10 mg daily for 21 days of each 28-day cycle since transplant. Her most recent serum protein electrophoresis shows no detectable M-spike, and bone marrow biopsy six months ago demonstrated minimal residual disease negativity by next-generation sequencing. She reports good tolerance with only mild fatigue on the last few days of each cycle.
She denies bone pain or new symptoms. Physical examination is normal. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin 12.3 g/dL, white blood cell count 4.2 × 10^9/L, and platelet count 128 × 10^9/L, all stable. Current medications include lenalidomide 10 mg daily for 21 days per cycle and acyclovir prophylaxis.
This represents sustained stringent complete response on lenalidomide maintenance with good tolerability. Continued maintenance therapy with routine monitoring every three months and consideration of MRD re-assessment at two years post-transplant is planned.

A 71-year-old man with chronic lymphocytic leukemia returns for follow-up on acalabrutinib. He was started on acalabrutinib 100 mg twice daily three years ago. His absolute lymphocyte count has decreased from 92 × 10^9/L to 11 × 10^9/L and has been stable for over a year. He reports good adherence and energy.
He has a history of atrial fibrillation managed with apixaban and metoprolol but has had no bleeding complications on acalabrutinib. Physical examination shows no palpable disease. Complete blood count demonstrates white blood cell count 14.2 × 10^9/L with absolute lymphocyte count 10.8 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, and platelet count 135 × 10^9/L.
Current medications include acalabrutinib 100 mg twice daily, apixaban, and metoprolol. This represents an excellent and durable response to acalabrutinib with good tolerability in a patient with prior atrial fibrillation. Continued therapy with routine monitoring is recommended.

A 54-year-old man with chronic phase CML returns for follow-up on asciminib. He was started on asciminib 40 mg twice daily 14 months ago after developing resistance to multiple prior TKIs including ponatinib. His most recent BCR-ABL1 IS level is 0.4%, improved from 8.2% at the start of asciminib. He reports good tolerance with only mild myalgias.
Physical examination is normal. Complete blood count shows white blood cell count 5.2 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, and platelet count 168 × 10^9/L. Current medications include asciminib 40 mg twice daily and low-dose aspirin.
This represents a good response to asciminib in a patient with multiply TKI-resistant CML. Continued therapy with close molecular monitoring every three months is planned, with consideration of allogeneic transplant evaluation if response is lost.

A 48-year-old woman with essential thrombocythemia returns for follow-up on hydroxyurea. She was started on hydroxyurea 1000 mg daily five years ago after recurrent transient ischemic attacks. Her platelet count has been stable between 320 and 420 × 10^9/L for the past two years. She reports good tolerance with no mouth ulcers or cytopenias.
Physical examination shows no splenomegaly. Laboratory studies demonstrate platelet count 365 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL, and white blood cell count 6.4 × 10^9/L. Current medications include hydroxyurea 1000 mg daily and low-dose aspirin.
This represents well-controlled essential thrombocythemia on hydroxyurea with stable platelet counts and good tolerability. Continued current therapy with platelet count monitoring every three to six months is recommended.

A 62-year-old woman with multiple myeloma returns for follow-up on ixazomib-lenalidomide-dexamethasone maintenance. She completed induction and autologous transplant two years ago and has been on this oral regimen for 18 months. Her M-spike has remained undetectable, and recent bone marrow biopsy showed sustained MRD negativity. She reports good tolerance with only mild fatigue and occasional diarrhea managed with supportive care.
Physical examination is normal. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, white blood cell count 4.8 × 10^9/L, and platelet count 142 × 10^9/L. Current medications include ixazomib 4 mg on days 1, 8, and 15, lenalidomide 10 mg daily for 21 days, and dexamethasone 20 mg weekly of each 28-day cycle.
This represents sustained MRD-negative complete response on an all-oral maintenance regimen with good tolerability. Continued therapy with routine monitoring every three months is planned.

A 71-year-old man with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and stage 3 chronic kidney disease was referred for evaluation of new anemia and progressive fatigue over the past four months. He reports mild dyspnea on exertion but denies melena, hematochezia, or other signs of gastrointestinal bleeding. He also denies fever, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss. Review of systems is negative for easy bruising or recurrent infections.

On examination, he has mild conjunctival pallor without lymphadenopathy or palpable splenomegaly. Laboratory studies show a white blood cell count of 5.6 × 10^9/L with absolute neutrophil count 3.4 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 9.3 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 93 fL, red cell distribution width 13.8%, and platelet count 172 × 10^9/L. Reticulocyte count is 2.1%. Iron studies reveal ferritin 185 ng/mL and transferrin saturation 28%. Vitamin B12 is 380 pg/mL with normal folate. LDH is mildly elevated at 245 U/L with normal haptoglobin. Serum creatinine remains stable at 1.55 mg/dL. No prior complete blood counts are available for comparison.

Current medications include lisinopril, atorvastatin, and low-dose aspirin. The differential diagnosis for his normocytic anemia includes myelodysplastic syndrome, anemia of chronic inflammation, and possible early primary marrow disorder. Peripheral smear review and consideration of bone marrow evaluation versus close laboratory monitoring are planned.

entities.verbatim: LDH is mildly elevated at 245 U/L

vignettes.text:
A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman was referred for evaluation of microcytic anemia and thrombocytosis identified on routine laboratory testing. She reports occasional fatigue but denies menorrhagia, gastrointestinal bleeding, or significant weight loss. She has a history of gastroesophageal reflux well controlled on a proton pump inhibitor and has no new abdominal symptoms.
Physical examination is unremarkable with no pallor, koilonychia, or splenomegaly. Laboratory evaluation demonstrates hemoglobin 10.3 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 69 fL, red cell distribution width 16.8%, and platelet count 492 × 10^9/L. White blood cell count is 7.1 × 10^9/L with normal differential. Ferritin is 19 ng/mL with transferrin saturation 8%. Peripheral blood smear shows hypochromic microcytic red cells and thrombocytosis without giant platelets. Vitamin B12 and folate are normal. No prior complete blood count from the past year is available for comparison.
Current medications include omeprazole 40 mg daily and lisinopril. The leading considerations are iron deficiency anemia, likely from occult gastrointestinal blood loss, with associated reactive thrombocytosis. Further endoscopic evaluation is being considered.

A 64-year-old man with a 30-pack-year smoking history was found to have persistent leukocytosis on pre-employment laboratory screening. Repeat complete blood count two weeks later showed similar results. He denies fever, cough, night sweats, weight loss, or any symptoms suggestive of infection or inflammation. He feels well and has no easy bruising or bleeding.
Physical examination reveals no splenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies demonstrate a white blood cell count of 16.8 × 10^9/L with absolute neutrophil count 13.5 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 14.2 g/dL, and platelet count 238 × 10^9/L. There is no basophilia or eosinophilia. Peripheral smear shows mature neutrophilia without left shift or dysplasia. No prior blood counts are available.
He takes no regular medications. The differential diagnosis includes a leukemoid reaction from occult infection or inflammation, chronic myeloid leukemia, and other myeloproliferative neoplasms. BCR-ABL1 testing by PCR and JAK2 V617F mutation analysis have been sent.

entities.verbatim: symptoms suggestive of infection or inflammation

vignettes.text:
A 67-year-old woman was noted to have an elevated absolute lymphocyte count on routine annual laboratory testing. She is entirely asymptomatic with no fatigue, night sweats, weight loss, or infections. She has a remote history of hypothyroidism on stable replacement but no other significant medical history.
Physical examination shows no lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. Laboratory evaluation reveals a white blood cell count of 9.4 × 10^9/L with absolute lymphocyte count 5.3 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL, and platelet count 218 × 10^9/L. The remainder of the differential is normal. Flow cytometry has been ordered to evaluate for a monoclonal B-cell population. No prior complete blood counts with differential are available in the record for comparison.
Current medications include levothyroxine. The differential includes reactive lymphocytosis versus a lymphoproliferative disorder such as monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis or early-stage chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Results of flow cytometry and repeat complete blood count in three months are pending.

A 49-year-old man was found to have marked thrombocytosis on routine preoperative laboratory testing for an elective hernia repair. A repeat complete blood count one week later confirmed the finding. He denies headaches, visual changes, erythromelalgia, or any vasomotor symptoms. He underwent a dental extraction approximately five weeks ago with normal healing and no excessive bleeding.
Physical examination shows no splenomegaly. Laboratory studies demonstrate a platelet count of 825 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL, white blood cell count 7.8 × 10^9/L with normal differential, and mean corpuscular volume 88 fL. Peripheral smear shows increased platelets without clumping or giant forms. JAK2 V617F mutation testing is pending.
Current medications include ibuprofen used occasionally for back pain. The differential diagnosis includes essential thrombocythemia versus reactive thrombocytosis, possibly related to recent minor surgery or occult iron deficiency. Iron studies have been ordered.

A 70-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis on low-dose methotrexate was referred for evaluation of unintentional weight loss, night sweats, and new splenomegaly. She reports approximately 10 pounds of weight loss over five months and drenching night sweats two to three nights per week for the past six weeks. She has had occasional low-grade fevers but no focal infectious symptoms.
On examination, the spleen is palpable 4 cm below the left costal margin without peripheral lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, white blood cell count 3.9 × 10^9/L with absolute neutrophil count 2.0 × 10^9/L, and platelet count 82 × 10^9/L. LDH is elevated at 310 U/L. Serum protein electrophoresis shows no monoclonal protein. CT imaging confirms splenomegaly without significant lymphadenopathy.
Current medications include methotrexate 15 mg weekly, folic acid, and ibuprofen as needed. The differential is broad and includes splenic marginal zone lymphoma, myelofibrosis, and other infiltrative or autoimmune processes. Bone marrow biopsy is being considered.

entities.verbatim: Serum protein electrophoresis shows no monoclonal protein

vignettes.text:
A 62-year-old man with obesity and a 25-pack-year smoking history was found to have an elevated hematocrit on routine laboratory testing. Repeat complete blood count confirmed the finding. He reports occasional morning headaches but denies visual changes, pruritus after showering, or erythromelalgia. He has loud snoring and daytime somnolence suggestive of possible obstructive sleep apnea but has not undergone formal testing.
Physical examination shows no splenomegaly or plethora. Laboratory studies demonstrate hemoglobin 17.9 g/dL, hematocrit 53.8%, white blood cell count 8.2 × 10^9/L, and platelet count 245 × 10^9/L. JAK2 V617F mutation is negative. Erythropoietin level is low-normal at 3.8 mIU/mL. No prior hematocrit values are available for comparison.
Current medications include lisinopril and atorvastatin. The differential diagnosis for his erythrocytosis includes secondary causes related to smoking and possible sleep apnea versus JAK2-negative polycythemia vera. Sleep study evaluation and consideration of phlebotomy versus observation pending further workup have been discussed.

A 55-year-old woman was noted to have thrombocytopenia on routine laboratory screening performed prior to a screening colonoscopy. Repeat complete blood count one week later showed a platelet count of 38 × 10^9/L. She reports mild easy bruising over the past two months but denies mucosal bleeding, heavy menstrual bleeding, or petechiae. She has no recent viral illnesses or new medications.
Physical examination reveals no splenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. Laboratory evaluation shows hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, white blood cell count 6.4 × 10^9/L with normal differential, and platelet count 41 × 10^9/L. Peripheral smear demonstrates decreased platelets without clumping or schistocytes. No prior platelet counts are available in the record.
Current medications include levothyroxine and a daily multivitamin. The differential for isolated thrombocytopenia includes immune thrombocytopenia, early myelodysplastic syndrome, and occult drug effect. Bone marrow biopsy versus close observation with repeat laboratory testing in one to two weeks is being considered.

A 68-year-old man with hypertension and type 2 diabetes presents with several months of progressive fatigue. Laboratory testing reveals new bicytopenia. He denies fever, night sweats, or significant weight loss. He reports no bleeding, easy bruising, or recurrent infections beyond occasional sinusitis.
On examination, he has mild pallor without lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. Laboratory studies demonstrate white blood cell count 3.1 × 10^9/L with absolute neutrophil count 1.3 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 9.7 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 101 fL, and platelet count 64 × 10^9/L. Reticulocyte count is 1.2%. Peripheral smear shows mild dysplastic changes in neutrophils and occasional hypogranular platelets. Vitamin B12 and folate are normal.
Current medications include metformin, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. The differential diagnosis includes myelodysplastic syndrome, aplastic anemia, and paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria. Peripheral blood flow cytometry for PNH and consideration of bone marrow biopsy are planned.

A 47-year-old man was found to have marked peripheral eosinophilia on laboratory testing obtained during evaluation of chronic fatigue and intermittent pruritus. He denies recent travel, new medications, or significant allergies beyond seasonal rhinitis. He has no history of asthma or sinus disease.
Physical examination is notable for scattered excoriations but no rash, lymphadenopathy, or organomegaly. Laboratory evaluation shows white blood cell count 10.8 × 10^9/L with absolute eosinophil count 3.4 × 10^9/L. Hemoglobin and platelet count are normal. Strongyloides serology, stool ova and parasite examination, and tryptase level have been sent. Vitamin B12 level is normal.
Current medications include cetirizine as needed for itching. The differential diagnosis for hypereosinophilia includes idiopathic hypereosinophilic syndrome, reactive causes (parasitic infection, drug reaction, or occult lymphoproliferative disorder), and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis. Further evaluation with CT imaging and possible bone marrow biopsy is planned.

A 59-year-old woman presents with several weeks of increasing fatigue and mild jaundice. She denies dark urine, fever, or recent illness. She has no history of autoimmune disease or prior blood transfusions.
Physical examination shows scleral icterus and mild pallor without lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. Laboratory studies demonstrate hemoglobin 8.8 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 98 fL, reticulocyte count 9.2%, LDH 385 U/L, haptoglobin <10 mg/dL, and indirect bilirubin 2.8 mg/dL. Direct antiglobulin test is positive for IgG. White blood cell and platelet counts are normal. Peripheral smear shows polychromasia and spherocytes.
Current medications include sertraline and omeprazole. The presentation is consistent with warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia. Further evaluation for underlying lymphoproliferative disorder or connective tissue disease is warranted, along with initiation of corticosteroid therapy.

A 72-year-old man with hypertension and mild chronic kidney disease presents with progressive fatigue and pallor over four months. Laboratory testing reveals new pancytopenia. He denies fever, night sweats, weight loss, or bleeding. He reports no recent infections beyond one episode of bronchitis three months ago.
Physical examination shows mild pallor without lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. Laboratory studies demonstrate white blood cell count 2.8 × 10^9/L with absolute neutrophil count 1.1 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 96 fL, and platelet count 58 × 10^9/L. Reticulocyte count is 0.8%. Peripheral smear shows mild dysplastic changes in neutrophils and occasional hypogranular platelets. Vitamin B12, folate, and iron studies are normal. LDH is mildly elevated.
Current medications include lisinopril and atorvastatin. The differential diagnosis includes myelodysplastic syndrome, aplastic anemia, and paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria. Peripheral blood flow cytometry for PNH clone and bone marrow biopsy with cytogenetics are planned.

A 58-year-old woman was found to have isolated neutropenia on routine laboratory testing. Repeat complete blood count three weeks later confirmed an absolute neutrophil count of 0.9 × 10^9/L. She denies recurrent infections, oral ulcers, or skin infections. She has no recent viral illnesses or new medications. She reports mild fatigue but otherwise feels well.
Physical examination is unremarkable with no lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. Laboratory evaluation shows white blood cell count 3.4 × 10^9/L with absolute neutrophil count 0.9 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL, and platelet count 198 × 10^9/L. Peripheral smear shows mature neutrophils without dysplasia. Antineutrophil antibody testing has been sent.
Current medications include sertraline and a daily multivitamin. The differential for chronic isolated neutropenia includes autoimmune neutropenia, chronic idiopathic neutropenia, early myelodysplastic syndrome, and drug effect. Bone marrow biopsy versus close observation with serial laboratory monitoring is being considered.

A 65-year-old man was referred for evaluation of anemia and an elevated total protein level found on routine laboratory testing. He reports mild fatigue but denies bone pain, weight loss, or night sweats. He has no history of kidney disease or hypercalcemia.
Physical examination is normal without lymphadenopathy or organomegaly. Laboratory studies demonstrate hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 92 fL, white blood cell count 6.2 × 10^9/L, and platelet count 215 × 10^9/L. Total protein is 8.9 g/dL with albumin 3.8 g/dL. Serum protein electrophoresis shows a polyclonal increase in gamma globulins without a discrete M-spike. Immunofixation is negative for monoclonal protein. Calcium and creatinine are normal.
Current medications include metformin and lisinopril. The differential includes polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia from chronic inflammation or liver disease versus an early or oligosecretory plasma cell disorder. Further evaluation with serum free light chains, urine protein electrophoresis, and consideration of bone marrow evaluation is planned.

A 61-year-old woman was found to have splenomegaly on imaging performed for unrelated abdominal pain. She reports no B symptoms, no early satiety, and no history of liver disease or infections. She has mild fatigue but attributes it to poor sleep.
Physical examination confirms a spleen palpable 3 cm below the left costal margin without peripheral lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies show white blood cell count 9.8 × 10^9/L with absolute lymphocyte count 4.2 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, and platelet count 145 × 10^9/L. Peripheral smear shows no abnormal lymphoid cells or rouleaux. Flow cytometry demonstrates a small population of CD5-negative, CD10-negative monoclonal B cells. JAK2 V617F is negative.
Current medications include levothyroxine. The differential for splenomegaly with mild lymphocytosis includes splenic marginal zone lymphoma, other indolent B-cell lymphomas, and myeloproliferative neoplasm. CT imaging of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis and bone marrow biopsy are planned.

A 69-year-old man with hypertension and gastroesophageal reflux was referred for evaluation of new macrocytic anemia. He reports fatigue and mild dyspnea on exertion over the past three months. He denies alcohol use, recent weight loss, or gastrointestinal bleeding. He takes omeprazole chronically but no other new medications.
Physical examination shows mild pallor without lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. Laboratory studies demonstrate hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 108 fL, white blood cell count 4.8 × 10^9/L, and platelet count 145 × 10^9/L. Reticulocyte count is 1.4%. Vitamin B12 is 320 pg/mL and folate is normal. LDH is mildly elevated. Peripheral smear shows macro-ovalocytes and occasional hypersegmented neutrophils. No prior complete blood counts are available.
Current medications include omeprazole, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. The differential diagnosis includes myelodysplastic syndrome, vitamin B12 deficiency despite borderline levels, medication effect, and hypothyroidism. Peripheral blood flow cytometry and consideration of bone marrow biopsy are planned.

A 58-year-old woman was found to have anemia and an abnormal peripheral smear on preoperative testing for elective surgery. She reports mild fatigue but denies bleeding, bruising, fever, or weight loss. She has no history of liver disease or malignancy.
Physical examination shows no splenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies demonstrate hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, white blood cell count 6.8 × 10^9/L, and platelet count 128 × 10^9/L. Peripheral smear reveals teardrop cells, occasional nucleated red blood cells, and mild leukoerythroblastosis. LDH is elevated at 290 U/L. JAK2 V617F mutation is negative.
Current medications include a daily multivitamin and ibuprofen as needed for osteoarthritis. The differential diagnosis includes primary myelofibrosis (JAK2-negative), myelophthisic process from occult malignancy or infection, and myelodysplastic syndrome with fibrosis. Bone marrow biopsy with reticulin staining and next-generation sequencing panel is recommended.
